questions.specification: RideFast is a ride-sharing company that uses mobile computing and peer-to-peer (P2P) networking to allow users to connect directly with drivers.

parts.content: Define peer-to-peer (P2P) networking and explain its relevance to RideFast.
parts.markscheme:
- Peer-to-peer (P2P) networking is a decentralized communications model in which each party (node) has the same capabilities and can initiate a communication session without the need for a permanent central server. <marks>1</marks>
- Relevance: It enables RideFast users to connect directly with drivers to coordinate rides, facilitating the primary service of the company. <marks>1</marks>
- Relevance: It allows for the efficient, localized distribution of real-time data (such as vehicle positions) directly between active participants in a specific area. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define P2P Networking
steps.content:
Peer-to-peer (P2P) networking is a **decentralized** communications model. Unlike a traditional client-server model where one central computer provides resources to others, in a P2P network, every computer or device (called a **node**) has equal status and capabilities. 

This means any node can initiate a communication session or share data directly with another node without needing to go through a permanent, central server. 

*This technical definition earns the first mark.*

highlights.text: peer-to-peer (P2P) networking

steps.title: Direct Communication in RideFast
steps.content:
In the context of **RideFast**, P2P networking is relevant because it allows the passenger's mobile device and the driver's mobile device to connect directly. 

This direct connection is essential for **coordinating the ride** in real-time, such as confirming the exact pickup spot or sending immediate status updates, which makes the service faster and more responsive for the user.

*Explaining this direct coordination earns the second mark.*

highlights.text: RideFast

steps.title: Real-time Data Sharing
steps.content:
P2P networking also facilitates the **efficient distribution of real-time data**, such as vehicle GPS positions. 

Instead of a central server processing and broadcasting the location of every single car to every single user, the network can share this data locally between active participants in a specific area. This ensures that users see accurate, low-latency movements of nearby drivers on their maps.

*Explaining the efficient sharing of real-time vehicle data earns the third mark.*

parts.content: Describe one benefit and one drawback of using P2P networking for RideFast’s operations.
parts.markscheme:
- Benefit: Scalability / Reduced Infrastructure Costs. <marks>1</marks>
- Because the network relies on the resources of the peers (drivers and users), the system can grow without a proportional increase in the need for expensive centralized server infrastructure. <marks>1</marks>
- Drawback: Security and Privacy risks. <marks>1</marks>
- Direct connections between untrusted peers make it difficult to implement centralized security monitoring, potentially exposing sensitive data like IP addresses or user locations to other participants. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the P2P infrastructure
steps.content: In a Peer-to-Peer (P2P) network, the workload is distributed among the participants (peers) rather than being concentrated in a single central server. For RideFast, this means the mobile devices of drivers and users act as both clients and "mini-servers" to facilitate connections.

highlights.text: peer-to-peer (P2P) networking

steps.title: Benefit: Reduced infrastructure costs
steps.content:
One significant benefit of this decentralized infrastructure is **scalability with reduced costs**. 

Because the network utilizes the processing power and bandwidth of the drivers' and users' own devices, RideFast does not need to invest in a massive, expensive central server farm to manage every connection. As the company grows and more users join, the network's resources grow naturally without a proportional increase in central infrastructure spending.

steps.title: Drawback: Security oversight
steps.content:
A major drawback of this approach is the **lack of centralized security and privacy oversight**. 

In a P2P setup, users connect directly to one another. This makes it difficult for RideFast to implement a "middle-man" security layer that monitors every interaction. This decentralization can expose sensitive data, such as a user's IP address or exact live location, directly to an untrusted peer (a driver or another user) without being scrubbed or masked by a central authority first.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To ensure you get all 4 marks, clearly link the benefit to resource sharing and the drawback to the loss of central control:

*   **Benefit (2 marks):** High scalability and lower costs because the system uses the resources of the peers (drivers/users) instead of centralized servers.
*   **Drawback (2 marks):** Increased security risks because direct connections between untrusted peers make it harder to enforce centralized monitoring and protect sensitive user data.

steps.title: Identify the P2P Context
steps.content:
In a peer-to-peer (P2P) network like RideFast's, the workload is distributed among "peers" (the mobile devices of drivers and riders) rather than relying solely on a central server. We will analyze this using a **Reliability and Management** approach to identify a specific benefit and drawback.

This topic is covered in **Topic 3.1: Networks**, specifically under the characteristics of different network types.

highlights.text: peer-to-peer (P2P) networking

steps.title: Benefit: High Availability
steps.content:
**Benefit:** High availability and reliability due to the lack of a single point of failure.

**Description:** Because the network is distributed across many peers, if one driver's device or a small group of nodes goes offline, the rest of the network continues to function. This ensures that the ride-sharing service remains available to other users, as there is no central hub that, if it crashed, would take down the entire connection system between all drivers and riders.

steps.title: Drawback: Management and Consistency
steps.content:
**Drawback:** Difficulty in managing software updates and maintaining data consistency.

**Description:** Since RideFast relies on hardware owned by the peers (the drivers), the company cannot easily control or "force" immediate software updates across all devices. This can lead to different versions of the app running simultaneously, making it difficult to ensure that data (such as GPS locations or fare calculations) is consistent and accurate across the entire distributed network.

steps.title: Mark Distribution Summary
steps.content:
To earn the full 4 marks, you must provide a clear benefit and a clear drawback, each supported by an explanatory point:

*   **[1 Mark]** Identifying a valid benefit (e.g., High availability/Reliability).
*   **[1 Mark]** Explaining how the distributed nature of P2P provides that benefit.
*   **[1 Mark]** Identifying a valid drawback (e.g., Management/Data consistency issues).
*   **[1 Mark]** Explaining why managing peers is harder than managing a centralized system.

parts.content: Explain one challenge RideFast may face in ensuring secure connections between users and drivers.
parts.markscheme:
- Challenge: Implementing robust encryption across a decentralized network. <marks>1</marks>
- Without a central authority to manage every connection, it is complex to ensure all peers use high-standard cryptographic handshakes to protect data. <marks>1</marks>
- This increases the vulnerability to man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks where communication between a user and a driver could be intercepted. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the Decentralized Challenge
steps.content: In a traditional system, a central server manages security. However, because RideFast uses a **peer-to-peer (P2P)** network, users and drivers connect directly. The primary challenge is implementing **robust encryption** across this decentralized environment where there isn't a single 'boss' to enforce security standards for every connection.

highlights.text: peer-to-peer (P2P)
Implementing robust encryption across a decentralized network.

steps.title: Analyze the Handshake Complexity
steps.content:
For a secure connection to occur, two devices must perform a "cryptographic handshake" to agree on how to scramble (encrypt) their data. In a decentralized network, there is no **central authority** to manage or verify these handshakes. 

This makes it technically complex to ensure that every individual peer is using a high-standard protocol, as the system relies on the software on each user's device to handle the security exchange correctly without a central gatekeeper. This concept relates to **Topic 3.1.11 (Network Security Methods)** in your syllabus.

highlights.text: Without a central authority to manage every connection, it is complex to ensure all peers use high-standard cryptographic handshakes to protect data.

steps.title: Assess the Security Risk
steps.content:
Because it is difficult to verify the identity of the peers without a central server, the system becomes more vulnerable to **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** attacks. 

In this scenario, an attacker could intercept the communication between a rider and a driver. Since the encryption management is decentralized, the attacker might trick the devices into using a weaker encryption key (or none at all), allowing them to steal sensitive information like GPS locations or payment details.

highlights.text: This increases the vulnerability to man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks where communication between a user and a driver could be intercepted.

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To get full marks on this question, you need to link the technical setup to the specific risk:

1.  **State the challenge:** Implementing encryption in a decentralized/P2P network.
2.  **Explain the technical difficulty:** The lack of a central authority to manage secure handshakes between peers.
3.  **Identify the consequence:** Increased risk of interception via MITM attacks.

steps.title: Analyze the P2P Environment
steps.content:
RideFast operates using a **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) network**, which means users and drivers connect directly to one another. Unlike traditional client-server models where a central server manages security, a P2P system is **decentralized**. 

You can review different network types in **Topic 3.1.1: Types of Networks** to understand why this architectural choice impacts security.

highlights.text: peer-to-peer (P2P) networking

steps.title: Identify the Authentication Challenge
steps.content:
The primary challenge is implementing **robust encryption and identity verification** across this decentralized network. 

In a standard system, a central authority (like a main server) validates the credentials of both parties. In a P2P setup, there is no "trusted third party" immediately available to verify that a driver or user is who they claim to be before the connection is established.

steps.title: Explain Technical Complexity
steps.content:
Because there is no central authority to manage every connection, it becomes highly complex to ensure that every peer (every mobile device) is using a high-standard **cryptographic handshake**. 

Without a managed protocol, it is difficult to distribute the "keys" necessary to encrypt the data, making it hard to prove the identity of the person on the other end of the mobile connection.

steps.title: Identify the Security Risk
steps.content:
This lack of verified identity increases the risk of **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attacks**. 

In this scenario, a "rogue peer" could impersonate a driver or user, intercepting sensitive communication (like location or payment data) between the two legitimate parties without them knowing. This relates to **Topic 3.1.11: Network Security Methods** regarding how unauthorized access is prevented.

steps.title: Identify the Peer Node Challenge
steps.content:
In a P2P network like RideFast, the "nodes" are the individual mobile devices of drivers and users. A significant challenge is that these nodes are **not centrally managed**, meaning the company cannot guarantee that every device is secure or up-to-date.

This is a core concept in **Topic 3.1.11 (Network Security Methods)**. Unlike a traditional server-client model where a central server is professionally maintained, P2P relies on the integrity of personal hardware.

highlights.text: mobile computing and peer-to-peer (P2P) networking

steps.title: Explain Security Inconsistency
steps.content:
Because the network is decentralized, RideFast faces the challenge of **inconsistent security patches**. 

Different users may be running older operating systems or have neglected security updates. If a driver's phone is infected with malware, it becomes a "vulnerable node." This makes it extremely difficult to implement robust, uniform encryption across the entire network because the endpoints (the phones) are not equally protected.

steps.title: Evaluate the Security Risk
steps.content:
Without a central authority to enforce high-standard cryptographic handshakes on every device, the connection becomes susceptible to **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attacks**.

If a peer node is compromised by malware, an attacker could intercept the communication between a driver and a rider. Even if the data is encrypted during transit, a vulnerable node might allow the attacker to access data at the point where it is decrypted on the device.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, your explanation should follow this logic:

1. **Challenge:** Implementing robust security across a decentralized network where nodes (mobile devices) have inconsistent security standards. ($1$ mark)
2. **Reason:** There is no central authority to manage every peer node's software state or ensure they are free of malware. ($1$ mark)
3. **Consequence:** This creates vulnerabilities, such as the risk of data interception or MITM attacks through these compromised endpoints. ($1$ mark)

parts.content: Describe how using mobile computing improves the convenience of RideFast’s services.
parts.markscheme:
- Ubiquity/Portability: Mobile computing allows users to request rides from any location with network coverage, providing greater flexibility than fixed systems. <marks>1</marks>
- Real-time GPS tracking: Integrated mobile sensors enable live vehicle tracking and accurate Estimated Time of Arrival (ETA) updates for the user. <marks>1</marks>
- Push Notifications: Mobile apps provide instant alerts about driver arrival, booking confirmations, or price changes, keeping the user informed in real-time. <marks>1</marks>
- Seamless mobile payments: In-app payment systems allow for quick, cashless transactions on-the-go, streamlining the overall user experience. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Define Mobility and Ubiquity
steps.content:
To answer this question, we first need to understand that **mobile computing** refers to the use of portable devices (like smartphones) that allow users to access data and services regardless of their physical location. 

In the context of **Topic 3.1.10 (Wireless Networks)** and **Topic 1.2.2 (Roles of Computers)**, we focus on how this portability removes the constraints of traditional, fixed computing systems.

steps.title: Explain Ubiquity of Access
steps.content:
The most significant improvement is **ubiquity**. Unlike traditional taxi services that might require calling from a landline or finding a physical 'taxi stand,' mobile computing allows users to request a RideFast ride from any location with network coverage.

This removes the need for **fixed terminals** (like a desktop computer at home). A user can be in a park, at a restaurant, or walking down a street and still access the service instantly.

highlights.text: Mobile computing allows users to request rides from any location with network coverage, providing greater flexibility than fixed systems.

steps.title: Utilize Real-time GPS Tracking
steps.content:
Mobile devices come equipped with **GPS sensors**. This portability means the device knows exactly where the user is. 

For RideFast, this improves convenience by allowing the app to automatically set the pickup location and providing **real-time tracking**. Users can see exactly where their driver is on a map and receive accurate Estimated Time of Arrival (ETA) updates, which reduces the uncertainty of waiting.

highlights.text: Integrated mobile sensors enable live vehicle tracking and accurate Estimated Time of Arrival (ETA) updates for the user.

steps.title: Leverage Instant Push Notifications
steps.content:
Because mobile devices are always 'on' and carried by the user, the service can use **push notifications**. 

This is more convenient than traditional systems because it provides instant alerts. Whether it’s a confirmation that a driver has accepted the request or an alert that the car has arrived outside, the user is kept informed in real-time without having to manually check the status.

highlights.text: Mobile apps provide instant alerts about driver arrival, booking confirmations, or price changes, keeping the user informed in real-time.

steps.title: Enable Seamless Mobile Payments
steps.content:
Finally, mobility allows for **integrated mobile payments**. Since the user's payment details are stored within the app on their portable device, the transaction is cashless and automatic.

This improves convenience by removing the need for physical currency or the manual processing of credit cards at the end of the trip, making the exit from the vehicle seamless and 'on-the-go.'

highlights.text: In-app payment systems allow for quick, cashless transactions on-the-go, streamlining the overall user experience.

methods.label: Focus on Real-time Sensors and GPS

steps.title: Define Mobile Computing Convenience
steps.content: In the context of RideFast, mobile computing refers to the use of smartphones and wireless networks to access services on the go. To earn the full 4 marks, we need to describe four distinct ways this technology makes the service more convenient for the user.

highlights.text: Describe how using mobile computing improves the convenience of RideFast’s services.

steps.title: Ubiquity and Portability
steps.content: The first major advantage is **ubiquity**. Because mobile devices are portable and rely on cellular networks, users are not tied to a desktop computer or a physical taxi stand. They can request a ride from virtually any location (e.g., a park, a friend's house, or a restaurant), providing much greater flexibility than traditional booking systems.

highlights.text: Mobile computing allows users to request rides from any location with network coverage, providing greater flexibility than fixed systems.

steps.title: Real-time GPS Sensor Integration
steps.content:
This is a core feature of mobile computing. Smartphones contain integrated **Global Positioning System (GPS) sensors**. These sensors constantly calculate the driver's exact coordinates. The RideFast app uses this data to:

1. Show the user a live map of the driver's vehicle moving in real-time.
2. Calculate an accurate **Estimated Time of Arrival (ETA)** based on current traffic and distance.

This removes the uncertainty of waiting for a ride, as users know exactly where their driver is at all times.

highlights.text: Integrated mobile sensors enable live vehicle tracking and accurate Estimated Time of Arrival (ETA) updates for the user.

steps.title: Automated Push Notifications
steps.content: Mobile computing allows the system to send **push notifications** directly to the user's lock screen. When the GPS sensor detects that the driver has arrived at the pickup point, the app automatically alerts the user. This means the user can wait comfortably indoors until the very last moment, rather than having to watch the street constantly.

highlights.text: Mobile apps provide instant alerts about driver arrival, booking confirmations, or price changes, keeping the user informed in real-time.

steps.title: Seamless Mobile Payments
steps.content: Finally, mobile computing enables **cashless transactions**. By securely storing payment details within the app, the transaction is handled automatically via the network once the trip is completed. This streamlines the experience because there is no need to carry cash, calculate tips manually, or wait for a card machine to process at the end of the journey.

highlights.text: In-app payment systems allow for quick, cashless transactions on-the-go, streamlining the overall user experience.

methods.label: Focus on Integrated Features and Automation

steps.title: Understanding Mobile Computing Benefits
steps.content:
To answer this, we need to look at how mobile devices (like smartphones) make the RideFast service easier to use compared to traditional taxi services. We will focus on how **integrated features** and **automation** create a seamless experience for the user.

In **Topic 1.2.2: Roles of Computers in a Networked World**, we explore how mobile technology provides constant connectivity, which is the foundation for all these convenience features.

steps.title: Ubiquity and Portability
steps.content: The first major improvement is **ubiquity**. Because mobile devices are portable, users can access the service from anywhere. Unlike a traditional system where you might need to find a landline or a specific physical taxi stand, mobile computing allows you to request a ride from any location as long as you have network coverage.

highlights.text: allows users to request rides from any location with network coverage

steps.title: Automated GPS Tracking
steps.content: Mobile computing utilizes **integrated sensors** like GPS. The app automatically shares your location with the driver and tracks the vehicle in real-time. This automation provides an accurate Estimated Time of Arrival (ETA), so you don't have to guess when your ride will show up.

highlights.text: Integrated mobile sensors enable live vehicle tracking

steps.title: Integrated Push Notifications
steps.content: A key automated feature is the use of **push notifications**. Instead of the user constantly checking the app for status updates, the system automatically sends instant alerts. These notifications inform the user when the driver has arrived or if there are changes to the booking, providing a hands-free way to stay updated.

highlights.text: instant alerts about driver arrival

steps.title: Seamless Integrated Payments
steps.content: Finally, convenience is improved through **integrated payment systems**. By automating the transaction within the app, the need for physical cash or manual card processing at the end of the ride is eliminated. This creates a 'frictionless' experience where the payment is handled automatically as soon as the trip is completed.

highlights.text: quick, cashless transactions on-the-go

questions.specification:
The infrastructure of the World Wide Web is increasingly moving toward decentralized models. Technologies such as BitTorrent and the InterPlanetary File System (IPFS) utilize peer-to-peer (P2P) protocols and distributed architectures to manage data storage and retrieval.

Unlike traditional web hosting, which relies on location-addressing (URLs pointing to specific servers), distributed approaches often use content-addressing where data is identified by its cryptographic hash. This shifting landscape impacts how files are found, distributed, and maintained across the globe.

parts.content: Explain how a BitTorrent client utilizes a "swarm" to facilitate the download of a large file.
parts.markscheme:
- The file is divided into many small, equal-sized segments or "pieces" <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The client connects to a tracker or uses a DHT to identify other peers who have some or all of the pieces (the swarm) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The client downloads different pieces from multiple peers simultaneously, while also uploading the pieces it has already finished to other peers in the swarm <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This distributed approach prevents the bottleneck associated with a single central server and increases download speed as more peers join <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State two differences between the traditional Client-Server model and the Peer-to-Peer (P2P) model for web-based applications.
parts.markscheme:
- In Client-Server, there is a clear distinction between the service provider (server) and the service requester (client), whereas in P2P, every node acts as both a client and a server (servent) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Client-Server models are centralized and represent a single point of failure, while P2P models are decentralized and offer higher fault tolerance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Resource scaling in Client-Server requires upgrading the central server's hardware, while in P2P, the system's capacity increases naturally as more nodes join the network <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
The InterPlanetary File System (IPFS) is a distributed web protocol that uses content-addressing rather than the location-addressing (HTTP) used by the traditional web.

Evaluate the impact of replacing location-addressed protocols with content-addressed protocols on the management and accessibility of web content.
parts.markscheme:
- Resilience/Persistence: Because files are identified by their content (hash), the same file can be retrieved from any node that has it, reducing reliance on a single server being online <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Efficiency: Content-addressing allows for deduplication; if multiple people upload the same file, it is only stored once, and users can potentially download it from a nearby peer (reducing latency) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Integrity/Verification: A hash-based address helps users verify that the retrieved content matches what was requested (tamper detection) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Content moderation/takedown difficulty: Distributed replicas can make it harder to remove illegal or harmful content compared with deleting it from a central host <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Deletion/privacy challenges: It may be difficult to ensure personal or outdated data is permanently removed if other nodes retain copies <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Availability incentives: Without a central owner, there is no guarantee peers will continue to host (“pin”) less popular content, which can reduce long-term availability <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Additional complexity/performance trade-off: Locating content may require extra mechanisms (e.g. peer discovery and routing via DHTs), which can add overhead compared with direct server addressing <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the role of a Distributed Hash Table (DHT) in maintaining a decentralized network.
parts.markscheme:
- A DHT provides a lookup service that maps keys (e.g. file identifiers) to values (e.g. which peers can provide the content) without requiring a central directory <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Each node in the network is responsible for a small portion of the keyspace, ensuring the workload is distributed <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Queries are routed through the network to the node(s) responsible for the relevant key(s), enabling peer discovery/content lookup <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The system can remain functional as nodes join and leave (churn) because responsibility for keys can be redistributed/updated <marks>1 mark</marks>

topic.title: C.3 Distributed approaches to the web
